data.title: Avient: Stable Is Good Enough For This Specialty Play At Current Levels
data.description:
During the market rout in March, I believed that specialty play Avient (NYSE:AVNT) was becoming more special. Following past disappointments, Avient has returned to growth in 2024 and sounded an upbeat tone for 2025.

After an uneventful first and second quarter, the company has maintained the full-year guidance in choppy operating conditions, which is good enough as shares kept being pressured, not having enjoyed the post-pandemic recovery like the rest of the market.

This underperformance creates a relatively appealing situation, as stable results are good enough given prevailing non-demanding valuations.

Avient: Innovative Material Solutions

Avient is an innovator of material solutions that aid customers in achieving objectives while simultaneously allowing for a sustainable world. Its specialty materials, polymer products, are used in a wide range of applications, many of which are used in everyday activities.

The company generates over $3 billion in sales, which are categorized under two separate segments. The largest is the CAI business, which stands for color, additives, and inks, a roughly $2 billion business. This is complemented by more than a billion SEM businesses, being specialty engineered materials.

The business is truly global, with some 40% of sales generated in North America, complemented by larger activities, notably in EMEA and Asia Pacific, with products used in a wide variety of applications, including consumer, industrial, transportation, building materials, and packaging applications.

While this looks like quite a stable and solid business, with EBITDA margins posted in the teens and targeted around 20%, there have been quite a few corporate announcements that have taken place in recent years. This followed the purchase of Clariant by PolyOne back in 2020, after which the combined entity was named Avient, as enthusiasm made that share peak at $60 in 2021.

The company bought the protective materials business from Dutch-based DSM in a $1.5 billion deal in 2022, after which distribution activities were sold in a $1 billion deal thereafter.

Disappointing

The company ended up not delivering on its targeted achievements, which it communicated around the time of all the foregoing M&A action, as 2023 sales ended up falling 7% to $3.1 billion, with earnings down in a more pronounced fashion to $2.36 per share. In fact, since 2021, shares have largely been trading in a $30-$50 trading range, with the share price clearly indicating that the performance was lagging.

Moreover, the company operated with over $1.5 billion in net debt, for a 3-times leverage ratio. The company guided for largely stagnant results in 2024 but beat expectations, with 2024 sales reported up 3% to $3.24 billion as adjusted earnings increased to $2.66 per share. Net debt was pretty stable at $1.5 billion, and with EBITDA reported at $526 million in 2024, leverage ratios fell just below 3 times.

However, for 2025, the company guided for EBITDA to improve to a midpoint of $555 million, with adjusted earnings seen at a midpoint of $2.82 per share. Trading down to $38 per share, valuation multiples had compressed to 13–14 times adjusted earnings, which felt like a low multiple amidst targeted improvements, and as a result, leverage was coming down as well.

Part of this might have to do with the fact that the company guided for a big gap between GAAP and adjusted earnings, already signaling a $71 million impairment charge and $86 million charge related to ERP systems, creating another year of a gap to GAAP earnings.

This left me to conclude that I was upbeat on the shares in the long haul but cautious to pull the trigger.

Expectations Fall Further

Shares have been pressured since the spring, driven by the tariff discussions and fear about the impact on the businesses. In the end, a $38 stock in March fell to lows of $28 per share, now trading at $33 per share following the release of the second quarter results.

The company posted first quarter sales at $827 million, down 0.2% on a reported basis but up 2% on an organic basis. The company posted flattish adjusted earnings at $0.76 per share, including a three-penny headwind from adverse currency moves, as the company maintained the guidance but actually posted a GAAP loss given the aforementioned charges.

On the first day of August, the company reported a near 2% increase in sales to $866 million, with currencies actually boosting growth by a percent as organic growth was reported at a not too inspiring 0.6%. Reported sales in the CAI segment were down 1% on a reported basis, driven by weaker consumer, transportation, and building materials demand. The performance at SEM was more impressive, with reported sales up 7%, driven by defense and healthcare, with the former sector now making up 7% of total sales.

Adjusted earnings came in at $0.80 per share, in line with the guidance, in fact a penny higher thanks to favorable currency moves. The company actually trimmed the midpoint of the full-year EBITDA guidance to $552 million, with earnings still seen at a midpoint of $2.82 per share. Net debt was reported at $1.55 billion, for a 2.8 times leverage ratio, as net debt actually ticked up a bit.

By now, the 92 million shares grant equity of the business a $3.0 billion valuation at $33 per share, for a near $4.6 billion enterprise valuation. This results in very reasonable multiples at around 12 times adjusted earnings, all while a recently raised dividend to $1.08 per share per annum (payable per quarter) has pushed up the dividend yield to 3.3% here.

Stable Is Good Enough

Given the non-demanding valuation, which keeps compressing while leverage ratios come down a bit amidst an improvement in EBITDA, expectations have cooled down as well. This means that the current flattish performance is enough, driven by the real fundamental valuation support here, as the operating performance is not inspiring yet, but that is okay at prevailing valuations.

While the macroenvironment remains uncertain, the company sees minimal direct impact from tariffs, driven by local sourcing practices. All this is relatively comforting, as investors get paid to wait, given a >3% dividend yield. Moreover, the earnings yield coming in around 8% here allows for additional funds used for investments and debt reduction, although the continued charges to GAAP earnings are not too welcome.

Investors have seen a sign of relief in response to the second quarter results, with shares trading up 5% to $33 per share, as flattish results are good enough. This makes me more upbeat on the business than I was in March, mostly driven by the pricing action with shares lagging the market in a big way and the second quarter results being largely uneventful.

Right now, I see an appeal on the increase, willing to buy into a modest position in anticipation of learning more about the business here, looking to add to this modest position on further dips and increase conviction.

data.title: 3 High-Yielding REITs I'm Buying
data.description:
Co-written by Austin Rogers

As any seasoned income investor can tell you, there are plenty of perils and pitfalls of high yield stock investing.

The market may not be perfectly efficient, but it isn't stupid either.

When a stock sells off to the point where it sports a high yield, that is a signal of the market's view that the company's growth prospects are poor and/or that it has an elevated level of risk.

Now, it is of course true that the market can and does overreact to both good news and bad news. Stocks very often overreact to the upside on positive sentiment while overreacting to the downside on negative sentiment. The market may be generally efficient, but it is still comprised of emotion-driven human beings.

Many contrarian and/or value investors make the mistake of jumping immediately to the bull case for a high yield stock without first understanding why the market has priced the stock at a high yield in the first place.

For example, many dividend investors look at the selloff of the blue-chip dividend aristocrat PepsiCo (PEP) over the last few years and conclude, after little to no investigation, that it is a great dip-buying opportunity.

It yields over 4%, which is rare for this snack & beverage giant. And it has grown its dividend over 50 consecutive years. And on top of that, PEP enjoys an enviably high credit rating of A+.

What's not to like?

Well, to be an intelligent contrarian, one must first understand why the market has recently discounted a blue-chip stock like PEP.

The reason, as pointed out in a recent article from Newsweek article titled "Ozempic Is Costing Grocery Stores Billions," is that soaring usage of GLP-1 drugs is majorly impacting sales of snacks and beverages like the products sold by PEP.

By suppressing hunger, GLP-1 drugs have a direct and negative effect on total food and beverage sales.

According to a self-reported survey from CNBC, GLP-1 users are more likely to spend less at restaurants and grocery stores after starting the drug than they are to spend more.

As more and more people begin using GLP-1 drugs, we should see this negative impact on food & beverage sales volumes increase over time.

A contrarian might then say that consumer packaged goods companies like PEP will be able to offset this by tactics like premiumization and shrinking package sizes.

We aren't making a bullish or bearish case for PEP here. We are simply illustrating that contrarian/value investing requires a lot more than merely identifying dips in stocks that have paid dividends for a long time. A high yield investor's research cannot begin and end with facts about the dividend. It has to also make the case that the market is wrong and has exaggerated the stock's fundamental downside.

That is exactly what we do at High Yield Landlord.

That is, we use our competency in commercial real estate to identify market mispricing in the REIT sector. This strategy has served us well over the last 7+ years, and we continue to follow it today.

With that in mind, let's look at three high yield REITs (VNQ) that we think the market has mispriced.

1. Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE)

This Class A life science REIT is a popular value stock on Seeking Alpha right now. There have been over 10 bullish articles on this stock in just the last few months.

We also explained why ARE is one of my favorite REIT investments in a recent article.

In that article, I did not shy away from the near-term challenges. There are plenty, ranging from oversupply (by far the biggest headwind) to a pharma-unfriendly presidential administration.

Since the life science market peak in 2022, the sheer amount of supply to hit the country's biggest life science markets has been astounding. This has resulted in double-digit declines in asking rent rates in all three of ARE's largest markets -- Boston, the Bay Area, and San Diego.

But there is an important point to emphasize here.

ARE owns Class A, state-of-the-art facilities primarily located in mega-campuses in these top research & development markets. The brand power of the "Alexandria" name is considerable. Tenants tend to opt for space in an ARE property before looking elsewhere.

Though availability in Tier 1 buildings (what ARE owns) has increased over the last few years as supply has come online, notice in the chart below that availability of Tier 1 buildings appears to have peaked and turned down while lower quality buildings continue to bleed tenancy.

The reason for this, as you can see from the above chart on the right, is that tenants are relocating from less desirable submarkets to more desirable submarkets.

Almost without exception, ARE's mega-campuses are the highest quality submarkets.

Given the aging population and ever-increasing demand for prescription drugs, we think tenant demand for life science space will gradually reduce the availability of Tier 1 space going forward, which should allow ARE to return to growth in a few years.

With that being our outlook, we think it makes no sense that ARE is today valued at 8.5x FFO. We could see it repricing to a 15x FFO multiple in 3-5 years, which gives it 75%+ upside in that timespan.

While we wait for that upside to manifest, we'll enjoy ARE's well-covered, 6.7%-yielding dividend.

2. Sila Realty Trust (SILA)

SILA is a lesser known REIT than ARE, but it is similarly compelling as a long-term investment opportunity.

The REIT specializes net leased, freestanding, mostly single-tenant healthcare properties across various parts of the care spectrum, from medical outpatient to surgery centers to inpatient rehab facilities.

"Net leased" means that the tenants are required to handle all or most property maintenance, insurance, and taxes. This feature significantly increases the stability and predictability of SILA's revenues and cash flows.

SILA sold off a bit recently on fears that government cuts to Medicaid funding would negatively impact some of its tenants, but the market seems to be shaking off such fears after concluding that little of this potential impact will flow through to SILA's performance.

But we still think SILA is deeply undervalued at an adjusted FFO multiple barely over 11x right now.

For one thing, we think the market undervalues SILA's defensiveness as a healthcare REIT. Healthcare real estate is at very low risk of disruption from AI, and the vast majority of healthcare provision will need to be in-person for the foreseeable future. Plus, aging demographics and a growing population of seniors will inevitably result in growing demand for healthcare.

The relatively small REIT owns 136 properties, 96% of which are currently leased, with a weighted average remaining lease term of almost 10 years and weighted average annual rent escalations of 2.2%.

Furthermore, SILA enjoys low debt levels, with a net debt to EBITDA ratio of 3.5x, and the REIT pays out only a little over 75% of its AFFO, making its dividend well-covered by cash flows. And the company has no debt maturities until 2028.

With a dividend yield of 6.4%, the market appears to be pricing SILA for basically zero growth. But the analyst consensus is that SILA generates bottom-line growth in the low- to mid-single-digits over the next several years. Even if SILA's bottom-line growth is no higher than its average annual contractual rent escalation of 2.2%, we still think the REIT looks undervalued.

We think SILA will earn a 13-14x multiple over time, which would imply an upside of 15-25% from here. While we wait for that upside, we'll continue to enjoy the REIT's very attractive and safe dividend.

3. Armada Hoffler Properties (AHH)

We think the selloff in this diversified East Coast REIT is a perfect example of how the market can overreact to the downside in the face of bad news.

We don't deny that there has been some bad news for AHH recently.

  1. Since the REIT is heavily invested in development projects that aren't yet generating revenues, its leverage levels look quite high. That makes the stock price particularly vulnerable to a "higher for longer" interest rate narrative.
  2. Apartment rent growth has slowed considerably, which negatively impacts the roughly 1/3rd of AHH's portfolio that is in multifamily properties.
  3. The REIT recently decided to issue a substantial sum of common stock to equitize the balance sheet rather than refinance loans at high interest rates. This was significantly dilutive (although any option to refinance maturing debts would have also been dilutive).
  4. Biggest of all, after a management transition at the beginning of this year, the REIT decided to cut its dividend by about 1/3rd. That catalyzed the ~30% selloff so far this year.

Shareholders hate dividend cuts and react more viscerally to them than, say, an earnings miss or an unexpected decision by management to sell a portion of the portfolio.

But as you can see from the chart above, the sellers now appear to have been exhausted, allowing AHH to find a base around $7.

That price represents a dirt cheap FFO multiple of about 6.7x, or ~9.5x AFFO (including maintenance capex).

We think this represents a clear overreaction by the market. There are several reasons why we think that AHH's fair valuation is significantly higher than its current market price.

For one thing, the majority of its portfolio are vibrant, high-occupancy, highly trafficked mixed-use centers in population-dense areas of the East Coast.

Only a tiny portion of AHH's office buildings are not located within these mixed-use centers. And the REIT's overall office segment is performing extraordinarily well with an occupancy rate in the high-90% area.

A prime example of the quality of these mixed-use centers is the Town Center of Virginia Beach, where the company has its headquarters as well as several future value-add opportunities:

Highly trafficked centers like these enjoy high walkability, persistently high occupancy rates, and high nighttime as well as daytime populations.

Second, the stabilized portfolio continues to enjoy strong fundamentals. All three segments (multifamily, retail, and office) continue to show positive same-store NOI growth.

Third, as mentioned above, AHH has a substantial value-creation pipeline, including ground-up development and redevelopment projects. About $4.5 million of additional revenue will commence this year, with more to come in future years. This will naturally bring down the REIT's debt-to-EBITDA levels over time.

Finally, we would point out that after the dividend cut, which washed out many shareholders, the new dividend rate of $0.14 per quarter is actually much safer and better covered by cash flows than the previous dividend. In other words, the dividend is safer and better covered today than before the dividend cut, but the stock price is lower.

We believe this represents a good opportunity to pick up shares of this ~8%-yielding REIT while it's on sale.

If management is able to gradually deleverage and strengthen the balance sheet while continuing to enjoy strong fundamental performance, as we think they will, we envision AHH's valuation roughly doubling from here.

Insider seem to agree, since there has been substantial insider buying recently.

Bottom Line

Investors ought to tread carefully when it comes to high yield stocks. As we said in the introduction, the market isn't stupid. When a stock is valued low enough to cause its yield to be relatively high, there are usually some good reasons for this.

But the market can and does overreact during big rallies or crashes. That happens all the time in the stock market.

At High Yield Landlord, we make it our singular goal to find these cases where quality real estate have been overly and irrationally discounted by the market. We think the three REITs above meet those criteria.

data.title: 6 Stocks I'm Buying As AI And Tariffs Battle For Market Dominance
data.description:
Are we in an artificial intelligence bubble?

Can it be a bubble if lots of people see it and identify it as a bubble? On the other hand, can humanity invent a revolutionary new technology without letting a speculative bubble form around it?

And aside from the AI bubble conundrum, where do tariffs come in? What will determine the course of the market going forward: AI or tariffs?

In this case, history is a guide but not necessarily a prophet.

For dividend-focused investors like myself, I think the surest wisdom is to stay the course. Keep accumulating. Maintain the momentum of your dividend snowball, even if the price appreciation of your portfolio is stalling out.

Here's the agenda for this week:

  • How AI and tariffs are creating winners and losers, haves and have-nots, in the current market.
  • Some ruminations on whether AI is indeed in a bubble right now, and how defensive yield stocks like REITs and utilities should perform in the foreseeable future if this is a bubble.
  • Some comments on each of the 6 dividend stocks and 1 active ETF on my buy list right now.

Onward!

AI & Tariffs Create Winners And Losers in 2025

In the macroeconomic chess game between artificial intelligence and tariffs, AI is unmistakably winning.

Overwhelmingly, the biggest driver of stock price appreciation today is the earnings (and expected future earnings) of AI-related companies.

Compare, for example, the year-to-date performance of the Roundhill Generative AI & Technology ETF (CHAT) against the Equal-Weight S&P 500 (RSP) and interest rate-sensitive real estate sector (VNQ):

The last few months have been like a Charles Dickens novel: "The best of times, the worst of times."

The market rally is definitively not broadening out, which is increasingly resulting in a bifurcation between the haves and have-nots.

The "haves" are almost all AI-related, while the "have-nots" are largely the tariff-exposed or rate-sensitive.

Axios recently attested that excluding the technology and communication services sectors, S&P 500 earnings are down 3% this year. So tariffs, economic uncertainty, weak lower-income consumers, and low housing turnover are taking their toll... just not with the biggest and most influential stocks in the market.

Certainly, some parts of the stock market are highly exposed to a hit from tariffs. Automakers like Ford (F), General Motors (GM), and Stellantis (STLA) are reporting huge, multi-billion dollar hits from tariff costs.

You can't raise taxes on $3.3 trillion of annual goods imports from a rate in the low single-digits to 18% without pain.

Most economic research, such as this recent report from the Yale Budget Lab, suggests that consumers will eventually bear the majority of the burden of these new tariffs. The Yale Budget Lab estimates that the average US household will eventually pay an extra $2,400 a year because of them.

But there's a lot of uncertainty in that word "eventually."

In the near-term, it looks like importers will be paying the majority of the tariffs, while consumers and foreign exporters foot only a minority of the bill.

That means margin contraction now, and a steady trickle of price hikes over the next few years (as long as the economy stays out of recession) that serve to rebuild margins but also put upward pressure on inflation metrics.

Meanwhile, AI-related stocks continue to surge upward, as indicated in the chart above.

To be clear, this isn't based solely on sentiment.

Meta Platforms (META) recently reported Q2 2025 EPS growth of 38% on the back of 22% revenue growth. And even as the tech giant accelerates its AI-related capex, it still generated $8.55 billion in free cash flow during the quarter.

Likewise, Microsoft (MSFT) recently reported fiscal Q4 EPS growth of 24% on the back of 18% revenue growth, driven primarily by cloud and AI-related operations. In its post-earnings rally, MSFT briefly surpassed $4 trillion in market cap for the first time.

AI is the real deal. The biggest players in this space are generating strong earnings growth from this revolutionary technology.

And they are spending heavily to keep the momentum going.

The four hyperscalers shown above are upping their capex budgets to over 50% of operating cash flow over the next few years, compared to about 35% over the last several years.

This bodes well for Nvidia (NVDA), whose state-of-the-art data center semiconductors remain in ultra-high demand among the hyperscalers. That is the primary reason it has performed about as well as MSFT and META so far this year.

The hyperscalers' spending is NVDA's income.

Is AI A Bubble?

Admittedly, I (as an income-oriented dividend growth investor) am biased. I am more prone to see AI as a bubble because of the recent underperformance of my distinctly tech-light portfolio.

That said, stock bubbles are very common during periods of rapid technological advancement. Think of the railroad mania in Britain in the 1840s, the post-war railroad speculative mania in the US in the late 1860s and early 1870s, the pre-Great Depression stock bubble of the 1920s, and of course the Internet bubble of the late-1990s.

One does not need to deny the revolutionary and productivity-enhancing nature of the new technology to see its associated stocks as being in a bubble.

And one of the hallmarks of a speculative asset bubble is that the new technology sucks up all the oxygen in the market, dominating investors' attention and dollars. Everything unassociated with the new technology is deemed unworthy of attention or investment.

That happened in the Dot Com bubble of the late 1990s.

The recently formed Nasdaq Index (QQQ) enjoyed an incredible run the last few years of the 90s and into the Spring of 2000. Meanwhile, the boring ol' real estate (VGSIX) and utilities (FKUTX) sectors sagged during this period as more and more capital got sucked into new-tech stocks.

During the bubble, the collective attitude of investors seemed to be that defensive yield stocks were just too boring to own. Why buy a stock offering total returns of maybe 8-10% per year when one can enjoy 20-30% per year (or more!) from high-growth tech stocks?

Why get rich slow when you can get rich quick?

But after the bubble burst and disillusionment took hold, investors rediscovered the value of these defensive yield investments, allowing them to greatly outperform in the first half of 2000s.

I think the collective attitude of investors is basically the same today as it was around the year 2000, especially for REITs (most of which have no claim to AI beneficiary status). REITs are just so boring compared to sexy, cutting-edge tech stocks. FOMO is a strong force at play in the market.

But at the same time, today, like the late 1990s, REITs are dramatically cheaper than the broader market:

The valuation gap today is reminiscent of the late 90s period, when a revolutionary new technology was all the rage and interest rates remained stubbornly elevated.

Utilities, on the other hand, are valued at around 19x earnings on average, making them around 3 points cheaper than the broader market in terms of P/E ratio.

The fundamentals for utilities may be better (especially the independent power producers but also regulated utilities) than for REITs, but REITs are meaningfully cheaper than utilities, giving them more potential upside from multiple expansion.

Plus, the next 2-3 years should offer a panoply of tailwinds for commercial real estate, including:

  1. Somewhat lower long-term interest rates (as a result of lower nominal GDP growth).
  2. Increased certainty pertaining to trade policy, which should facilitate a rebound in job growth and tenant demand.
  3. A huge drop in deliveries of new supply, especially for the oversupplied industrial and residential sectors.

Notice also that starts of senior housing properties have been very low over the last several years, despite the clear resurgence of demand.

This explains why senior housing REITs like Welltower (WELL) and American Healthcare REIT (AHR) have absolutely crushed the S&P 500 (SPY) so far this year:

WELL and AHR demonstrate that REITs with powerful organic growth drivers are still capable of strong price performance, even if the rest of the real estate sector is deemed boring.

Perhaps it is merely apophenia (the human tendency to see patterns amid randomness and unrelated data) tricking me into seeing a resemblance between the late 90s and today, but the similarities do seem obvious and striking to me.

Maybe I'm wrong and growthy tech will continue to dominate the market for multiple years to come. But if I'm onto something, then REITs and utilities should be big winners over the next 2-3 years.

My Dividend Growth Buy List Today

Turning now to my boring, dividend growth stock buy list, I note that there are 6 individual names I hope to buy at least some shares in this week, plus an actively managed dividend growth ETF in which I'd like to begin building a position as well.

STOCKS Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) 3.4% Mid- to High-Single-Digit
American Tower (AMT) 3.3% Mid- to High-Single-Digit
Artesian Resources (ARTNA) 3.7% Mid-Single-Digit
EastGroup Properties (EGP) 3.4% High-Single-Digit
H2O America (HTO) 3.4% Mid-Single-Digit
Public Storage (PSA) 4.3% Mid-Single-Digit
ETFs
Capital Group Dividend Growers ETF (CGDG) 2.3% High-Single-Digit (Or Better)

Four of the individual names on my buy list are high-quality REITs, while the remaining two are high-quality water utilities.

American Homes 4 Rent

I continue to think that AMH's internal build-to-rent business segment is an underappreciated competitive advantage. It has allowed the REIT to continue growing its portfolio of single-family rentals while many of its corporate landlord peers have been net sellers of homes. The ability to internally develop homes at a relatively high stabilized yield gives AMH the capability to accretively grow its asset base in almost any environment.

Other reasons to like AMH include demographics (Millennials entering their prime single-family years of life), homebuying unaffordability, scale advantages, and balance sheet strength.

The average 20% down payment now represents ~100% of the median household income, while the average mortgage payment is over 30% of household income.

If homebuying affordability remains this low, because of home prices or mortgage rates or both, then AMH should continue to enjoy robust demand for many years to come.

American Tower

The market didn't like something in AMT's Q2 earnings report, perhaps the delayed domestic tower billing. Or maybe AMT's outlook simply wasn't as positive as the market had been pricing in.

In any case, I see this dip as an attractive buying opportunity. The mid- to high-single-digit bottom-line growth outlook remains intact, CoreSite continues to generate double-digit revenue growth and is a second-order beneficiary of AI, and the REIT just earned a credit upgrade to BBB+ from S&P.

Artesian Resources

This small, family-controlled Delaware water utility is conservatively managed with the goal of slow-and-steady growth and 4%-per-year dividend raises. The company boasts a 32-year dividend growth track record. It's boring, but it's consistent and reliable.

I believe boring, consistent, and reliable dividend growers have a proper role to play in a diversified dividend growth portfolio.

EastGroup Properties

I think industrial REITs, including EGP, are in a dip right now because of (1) elevated levels of new supply additions that will taper off going forward and (2) sluggish tenant demand due to the cloud of uncertainty around trade policy. Both are temporary headwinds, in my view.

EGP is low-leveraged and well-positioned with its last-mile industrial facilities in fast-growing Sunbelt markets.

H2O America

Compared to ARTNA, HTO is a much larger and more geographically diversified water utility holding company. And as many smaller water utilities struggle with the financial burden of keeping up the requisite maintenance and replacement of aging infrastructure, HTO stands to benefit as a consolidator.

HTO owns four regional water utilities: Connecticut Water, Maine Water, San Jose Water, and Texas Water. Recently, HTO more than doubled its presence in Texas by acquiring Quadvest, a Houston-based water utility. This acquisition puts HTO on pace to become the second largest water utility provider in the state of Texas by 2028.

As a Texas whose eyebrows are raised at almost every monthly water bill, this expansion in Texas makes a lot of sense to me.

HTO aims to grow adjusted EPS by 5-7% per year, and I would expect the dividend to grow in that range as well.

Public Storage

PSA sold off hard after releasing Q2 earnings, despite the self-storage giant bumping up the lower end of its bottom-line guidance. The REIT is also in acquisition mode, expecting to complete over $1 billion of property acquisitions this year. Even so, occupancy dropped a bit from 93% in Q2 2024 to 92.6% in Q2 2025, and core FFO per share growth is guided to be flat to barely positive this year.

My guess is that PSA is a victim of "higher for longer" interest rates, because higher rates keep homebuying unaffordable, which keeps housing turnover very low. Housing turnover is a primary driver of storage space leasing.

As a long-term investment, now looks like a great time to buy this low-leveraged, A rated, conservatively managed, best-in-class, 4.4%-yielding REIT. It is now valued at about 17x AFFO, which is meaningfully below its historically average 20-21x multiple.

Capital Group Dividend Growers ETF

It is difficult to analyze actively managed ETFs, because there's no stock-picking methodology to cover, and the top holdings could change at any time as the portfolio managers decide to buy or sell.

Other than pointing out its 0.47% expense ratio, which is rather high but not as high as some other actively managed dividend ETFs, there are a few other things to note.

First, CGDG is a globally diversified portfolio, with 47% of holdings in the US, 29% in Europe, 15% in Asia, and the rest elsewhere.

Second, one can read up on the managers, who in this case include Philip Winston, Steve Watson, and Grant Cambridge.

Third, you can look at the current top holdings to get a sense of management's capital allocation strategy.

Note the 3.8% weighting in cash, which proves useful for opportunistic dip-buying.

Fourth, to borrow an ancient phrase, "you will know them by their fruits" -- that is, their performance.

Since its inception and especially since the market trough in early April, CGDG has performed strikingly well compared to peer ETFs like the Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM) and the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD):

The managers' ability to hold cash for opportunistic dip-buying probably played a role in CGDG's outperformance since the April market lows.

While I maintain positions in passive ETFs like SCHD, iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO), and iShares Core High Dividend ETF (HDV), I believe well-managed active ETFs like CGDG make good diversifiers and are worth the additional expenses.

I went more in depth into my thinking behind portfolio allocations to active ETFs in "Dividend Growth Is My Antidote to Uncertainty."

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Relay Therapeutics: What's Next For H2 2025?
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

I have covered Relay Therapeutics (NASDAQ:RLAY) with muted optimism, with my last article upgrading sentiment to a buy after a steep valuation decline that I did not feel was justified. Since then, we've had ASCO and a quarterly update to digest. So let's take another look at how the RLAY investment thesis is shaping up after the halfway mark of the year.

Pipeline Updates

RLY-2608

After outlicensing the FGFR2 inhibitor lirafugratinib to Elevar Therapeutics, RLAY's developmental efforts have been focused almost entirely on the PI3K-alpha inhibitor RLY-2608. The main shot on goal here is in the space of oncology, with a focus on PIK3CA-mutated breast cancer. The company hopes that by targeting the variant form of PI3K, they can avoid the tolerability issues that have dogged agents in this class for years.

RLAY presented an update of their ReDiscover study at ASCO 2025, demonstrating a median PFS of 9.2 months across all dose levels (11.4 months in the 32 patients who received the recommended phase 2 dose). In this study, RLY-2608 was given in combination with fulvestrant to patients with advanced, hormone receptor-positive breast cancer after progression on endocrine therapy plus a CDK4/6 inhibitor.

These findings continue to echo those observed in the INAVO-120 study, a positive phase 3 trial establishing the addition of the PI3K inhibitor inavolisib to first-line therapy for patients with advanced breast cancer and a PIK3CA mutation. Also, the CAPItello-291 study assessed the addition of capivasertib to fulvestrant as second-line treatment, demonstrating a median PFS of 7.2 months.

The phase 3 ReDiscover-2 trial is set to begin imminently, and it will compare RLY-2608 directly against capivasertib in patients with previously treated, hormone-positive metastatic breast cancer.

Financial Overview

As of their latest quarterly filing, RLAY held $93.5 million in cash and equivalents, along with another $616.8 million in investments. They had $7.7 million from license revenue while incurring $84.9 million in operating expenses. After interest, the net loss for the quarter was $77.1 million.

Negative cash flow was $73.2 million for the quarter, and at this burn rate, the implied cash runway for RLAY is between 9 and 10 quarters, but the company guides that their cost-saving steps should be sufficient to extend their runway "into 2029".

Strengths and Risks

Strength: Cash sufficient to get their phase 3 trial off the ground and perhaps into top-line data

After their outlicensing to Elevar, RLAY maintains the rather healthy liquidity needed to fund critical, ongoing development. They believe that the funding they have will be sufficient to carry them all the way to a top-line data readout of the pivotal ReDiscover-2 trial, which could very well be a game-changing catalyst in itself.

And that's before touching on the potential of RLY-2608 in vascular malformations, as well as their preclinical pipeline that could end up moving to the clinic in relatively short order.

Risk: No phase 3 trial to date (as of this writing)

All that said, RLAY has guided that they would begin their phase 3 trial in mid-2025, and we are past the midpoint of the year. Every day's delay means that the runway needs to be stretched that much further in order to maintain solvency through a key data readout.

Strength/Risk: A big market, but with big competition

We are no longer in the days of PI3K inhibitors that come with unacceptable, unmanageable toxicity, ala the days of idelalisib. In the space of breast cancer alone, capivasertib and inavolisib are both PI3K inhibitors with a reasonable pedigree, and even though capivasertib's peak sales guidance has been revised downward, the drug still commanded $430 million in sales in 2024. A lot is riding on the success of ReDiscover-2.

Bottom-Line Summary

Moving into the back half of the year, RLAY continues to execute on a number of fronts, providing an important update for ReDiscover and working hard to get their registrational trial launched. The sooner this occurs, the sooner we will have some kind of guidance on when to expect pivotal trial data.

Meanwhile, at the time of writing, the company is valued substantially below its total liquidity, indicating that the market does not feel the pipeline has value at this time. Certainly, the company will continue to burn cash for quite some time, but I continue to maintain that their pipeline is far from worthless, particularly if they can come out and beat capivasertib in ReDiscover-2.

I maintain my "Buy" sentiment based on the upcoming initiation of a phase 3 trial, positive data updates, and safe liquidity sufficient to fund operations for several more years before RLAY needs to raise funds again. It is very much worth continuing to watch, if not consider adding a small position in a risk-tolerant portfolio.

data.title: Weekly Indicators: Privately Sourced And Government Data Just Got Critically Important
data.description:
Purpose

I look at the high frequency weekly indicators because while they can be very noisy, they provide a good nowcast of the economy, and will telegraph the maintenance or change in the economy well before monthly or quarterly data is available. They are also an excellent way to "mark your beliefs to market." In general, I go in order of long leading indicators, then short leading indicators, then coincident indicators.

A Note on Methodology

Data is presented in a "just the facts, ma'am" format with a minimum of commentary so that bias is minimized.

Where relevant, I include 12-month highs and lows in the data in parentheses to the right. All data taken from St. Louis FRED unless otherwise linked.

A few items (e.g., Financial Conditions indexes, regional Fed indexes, stock prices, the yield curve) have their own metrics based on long-term studies of their behavior.

Where data is seasonally adjusted, generally it is scored positively if it is within the top 1/3 of that range, negative in the bottom 1/3, and neutral in between. Where it is not seasonally adjusted, and there are seasonal issues, waiting for the YoY change to change sign will lag the turning point. Thus I make use of a convention: data is scored neutral if it is less than 1/2 as positive/negative as at its 12-month extreme.

With long leading indicators, which by definition turn at least 12 months before a turning point in the economy as a whole, there is an additional rule: data is automatically negative if, during an expansion, it has not made a new peak in the past year, with the sole exception that it is scored neutral if it is moving in the right direction and is close to making a new high.

For all series where a graph is available, I have provided a link to where the relevant graph can be found.

Recap of Monthly Reports

July data started out with a very poor employment report, including the most negative revisions outside COVID in several decades. The ISM manufacturing index and its new orders subindex also declined further into contraction.

June data included a further downturn in construction spending. The Michigan consumer sentiment survey indicated an upturn as to current conditions but a downturn in the outlook. Real personal income was flat, while real spending increased slightly. Housing prices as measured by both the FHFA and Case Shiller declined slightly. Job openings, hires, quits, and layoffs all declined slightly.

In the rear view mirror, Q2 real GDP rebounded due to inventories and a downturn in imports, while “core” GDP, real final sales to domestic purchasers, slowed to the lowest growth in several years.

Long leading indicators

Interest rates and credit spreads

Rates

  • BAA corporate bond index 6.04%, down -0.07% w/w (1-yr range: 5.33-6.43)
  • 10-year Treasury bonds 4.38%, down -0.17% w/w (3.66-4.98)
  • Credit spread 1.66%, down -0.10% w/w (1.36-2.02).

(Graph at Moody's Seasoned Baa Corporate Bond Yield | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

Yield curve

  • 10 year minus 2 year: +0.53%, up +0.07% w/w (-1.07 - +0.53)
  • 10 year minus 3 month: -0.06%, down -0.12% w/w (-1.65 - 0.17)
  • 2 year minus Fed funds: -0.65%, down -0.24% w/w.

(Graph at 10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity Minus 2-Year Treasury Constant Maturity | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

30-Year conventional mortgage rate (from Mortgage News Daily)

  • 6.63%, down -0.18% w/w (6.11-7.26).

Treasury and corporate bond yields, as well as mortgage rates, remain lower than their near 12 month highs of several months ago. The spreads between Treasuries and investment grade and high yield bonds continue to show little change.

The yield curve measures remain where they have been, more or less, for months, with the 10-2 year spread being the most positive, and the 2 year vs. Fed funds being the most negative. The 10 year minus 3 month is retreated to negative as well.

Housing

Mortgage applications (from the Mortgage Bankers Association)

  • Purchase apps down -6% to 144 (114-163) [SA]
  • Purchase apps 4 wk avg. down -2 to 153 [SA]
  • Purchase apps YoY% up +17% [NSA]
  • Purchase apps YoY 4 wk avg. +17% [NSA]
  • Refi apps down -1% w/w [SA]
  • Refi apps YoY up +30% [SA].

*[SA] = seasonally adjusted, [NSA] = not seasonally adjusted

Real Estate Loans (from the FRB)

  • Up less than +0.1% w/w
  • Up +1.4% YoY (tied for multi-year low).

(Graph at Real Estate Loans, All Commercial Banks | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

Both purchase mortgage and refinancing applications have been volatile, but within a limited range over the past 18 months. Both are now substantially higher YoY, but remain close to their longer term bottoms, so they score neutral.

In 2023 real estate loans sank below 1/2 of their 12 month high, the last housing indicator to turn negative, and have generally continued to worsen, once again tying their post-pandemic low once again.

Money supply

The Federal Reserve has discontinued this weekly series. Data is now only released monthly. June data was just released last week:

  • M1 m/m up +0.6%, YoY Real M1 up +1.6% (3 year high) (worst: -1.4% 8/23)
  • M2 m/m up +0.6%, YoY Real M2 up +1.9% (2.1% June 2025 3 year high) (worst -8.6% 9/23).

No recession has happened without a YoY real M1 negative, or YoY real M2 below +2.5%. Real M2 fell below that threshold in March 2022. Real M1 also turned negative as of May 2022. In the past, when real money supply has rebounded to the levels it is at now, it is either during the latter part of a recession, or in the period of recovery, so both of these are positive long leading metrics now.

Corporate profits (56% actual + 44% estimated) from I/B/E/S via FactSet at p. 34)

  • Q2 up +1.30 to 64.97, up +2.0% q/q.

FactSet estimates earnings, which are replaced by actual earnings as they are reported, and are updated weekly. The "neutral" band is +/-3%. I also average the previous two quarters together, until at least 100 companies have actually reported. In Q1 earnings wound up lower by more than -3%, which turned the rating negative. Now that enough companies have reported for Q2, and it currently shows an increase, the rating has changed to neutral.

Credit conditions (from the Chicago Fed)

  • Financial Conditions Index down -0.02 ( looser) to -0.57 (-0.42 - -0.67)
  • Adjusted Index (removing background economic conditions) down -0.01 (looser) to -0.52 (-0.34 - -0.61)
  • Leverage subindex down -0.03 (looser) to -0.55 (+0.13 - -0.76).

In these indexes, lower = better for the economy. The Chicago Fed's Adjusted Index's real break-even point is roughly -0.25. In the leverage index, a negative number is good, a positive poor. The historical breakeven point has been -0.5 for the unadjusted Index. After briefly deteriorating to neutral several months ago, all three were recently solidly positive, but the unadjusted index remains neutral.

Short leading indicators

Economic Indicators from the late Jeff Miller’s “Weighing the Week Ahead”

  • Miller Score (formerly "C-Score"): down -5 to 416, up +15 m/m (232 8/30/24 - 421 tied with 5/16/25) Components: Yield curve 67 Phillips curve 354
  • St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index: down -0.0785 to -0.7950 (+0.5569 4/11/25 - -.9773 1/24/25)
  • BCIp from Georg Vrba: up +3.5 to +46.0 as of 7/30/25 iM's Business Cycle Index (100 is max value, below 25 is recession signal averaging 20 weeks ahead).

The Miller Score is designed to look 52 weeks ahead for whether or not a recession is possible. Any score over 500 means no recession. This number fell below that threshold at the beginning of August 2021, so not only is it negative, but we are now years into the “recession eligible” time period. This indicator is heavily influenced by both the yield curve and the still very low unemployment rate vs. gas prices (expecting a reversal) which explains why it remains negative. My colleague who compiles this score began disaggregating the two components several months ago.

The St. Louis Financial Stress index is one where a negative score is a positive for the economy, and during its limited existence, has risen above zero before a recession by less than one year. It remained very positive until briefly in April. It then recovered and remains near its best post-pandemic readings.

For over a year IM has stated that their system does not forecast a recession, although in the last month it has edged closer to their threshold.

Trade weighted US$

  • Up +8.02 to 120-41 w/w, down -2.9% YoY (last week) (broad) (118.76 - 129.67) (Graph at Nominal Broad U.S. Dollar Index
  • Up +1.11 to 98.76 w/w, down -4.3% YoY (major currencies) (graph at link) (99.01- 109.66).

The neutral range for the US$ is between unchanged to 5% higher YoY. At 5% higher or more YoY, the strength suppresses exports and incentivizes imports, a negative for the economy. The trend in these measures had been from below 0 (thus positive) to neutral, to above 5% (negative) for 18 months, until the beginning of this year. Both measures are lower YoY now, so are positives.

Commodity prices

Bloomberg Commodity Index

  • Down -2.85 to 100.62 (93.30 - 107.71)
  • Up +6.2% YoY.

(Graph at https://www.marketwatch.com/investing/index/bcom?countrycode=xx.)

Bloomberg Industrial metals ETF (from Bloomberg) (graph at link)

  • 139.44, down -18.26 w/w (130.47 -173.08 )
  • Down -1.3% YoY.

Beginning three months ago the industrial measure gradually weakened from positive to negative. Then the broad commodity index declined sharply into negative territory, but then rebounded into neutral range. By contrast, the industrial metals measure remained very negative. This can be attributed to whether or not oil is included among the commodities tracked. This week the industrial measure turned lower YoY, and so negative. Please note, importantly, that if commodities were not priced in US$ terms, they would not nearly have risen so much YoY.

Stock prices S&P 500 (from CNBC) (graph at link)

  • Down -2.4% to 6238.01.

In early April stocks made a 12 month low, but this was more than 3 months ago. This month, several times this week, they have made several new all-time highs. As a result this indicator changed from neutral to positive.

Regional Fed New Orders Indexes

(*indicates report this week)

  • Empire State, up +16.2 to 2.0
  • Philly up +16.1 to 18.4
  • Richmond down -13 to -25
  • Kansas City up +13 to +2
  • *Dallas up +3.7 to -3.6
  • Month-over-month rolling average: up +1 to -1.

The regional average is more volatile than the ISM manufacturing index, but usually correctly forecasts its month-over-month direction. Since spring 2022, these gradually declined to neutral and then negative. The indexes varied sharply last year, but recently became more variable, first turning neutral and then one month+ ago to positive. This may have reflected manufacturers’ attempting to front-run new tariffs - which would account to the sharp reversals beginning in March.

In April, I wrote that

“I am strongly considering adding the regional service indexes this month, to see if the consumer segment of the economy suddenly shrinks in as close to real time as reasonably available.”

Those reports are posted below, with new reports posted in the past week noted with an *:

  • Empire State up +3.9 to -9.3
  • Philly up +1.0 to -16.3
  • Richmond up +6 to -8
  • Kansas City down -5 to -2
  • *Dallas up +6.4 to +2.0
  • Average: up +1 to -7.

Contra the above non-manufacturing reports, the ISM non-manufacturing report turned more positive in April. It has stabilized at negative levels since May.

Employment metrics

Initial jobless claims

  • 218,000, up +1,000 w/w, down -12.1% YoY
  • 4-week average 221,000, down -3,500 w/w, down -6.6% YoY.

(Graph at St. Louis FRED.)

Jobless claims were positive for most of 2024, except for hurricane-influenced weeks during the autumn. They subsequently returned to neutral, then briefly positive, before turning back to neutral. We have had very good reports for the last five weeks, and last week this indicator has improved to positive.

Temporary staffing index (from the American Staffing Association) (graph at link)

  • Up +1 to 85 w/w
  • Down -2.7% YoY (low -13.6%- high -4.7% 7/18/25).

This series has been negative since early 2023. YoY comparisons continued to be negative throughout 2024. It remains frankly recessionary. I suspect this has been a secular change and giving a false signal as a result. Relatively speaking, the YoY reading has been trending “less bad” for the past number of months, and finally, since it is now more than half the way to unchanged on a YoY basis, to neutral as of earlier this year.

Tax Withholding (from the Department of the Treasury)

  • $245.5 B for the last 20 reporting days vs. $228.8 B last year, up +$16.7 B or +7.3%.

These were very positive for almost all of 2024, and with the exception of seven weeks (four after an April 15 anomaly), the trend has continued this year.

Oil prices and usage (from the E.I.A.)

  • Oil up +$2.25 to $67.31 w/w, down -6.0% YoY ($58.21 - $83.88)
  • Gas prices unchanged at $3.12 w/w, down -$0.36 YoY
  • Usage 4-week average down -3.0% YoY.

(Information can be found at “This Week In Petroleum” at the E.I.A.’s website.)

Oil prices first declined into the bottom 1/3rd of their 3 year range, and so turned positive late in 2024, and then declined even further earlier this year. After the Israeli-Iran hostilities last month, oil prices increased, but have since receded back to their more normal recent range.

Gas prices also recently made new 3 year lows, and so are also positive. Last year, mileage driven had been generally negative. It turned neutral and then positive earlier this year, but negative again last week. Since March of this year it has oscillated between positive and negative.

Note: given this measure’s extreme volatility, I believe the best measure is against their 3 year average.

Bank lending rates

  • 4.39% Secured Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR), up +0.09% w/w.

The TED Spread has been discontinued, and as of last September so was LIBOR. At the suggestion of a reader, I began to track the SOFR instead. Unfortunately, SOFR has only been in existence since 2018, so there is no track record has to how it might behave around normal recessions (vs. the pandemic). Over the past 5 years, it does appear to have matched the trend in LIBOR. Given the alternatives, I am now including it in my list of indicators in the conclusion.

Coincident indicators

Tariff payments (from the Department of the Treasury)

  • June 2025 vs. June 2024: $28.0 B vs. $7.6 B (+$20.6 B)
  • July 1-30 2025 vs. July 1-16 2024: $29.0 B vs. $8.6 B (+20.4 B).

I have inaugurated this measure temporarily since other sources, such as inferences from the CPI and PPI, are imprecise and speculative so far. The above information comes directly from the Daily Treasury Statement. These fees are either absorbed by the importer or passed on to the consumer, or some proportion of each, and are removed from potential consumption on other items.

St. Louis FRED Weekly Economic Index

  • Up +0.31 to 2.56 w/w (Low 1.65 November 2, 2024 - high 3.21 November 23, 2024).

This measure was in the neutral range during most of early 2024, but generally positive (above 2.00) since then, and came very close to setting a new 12 month high three months ago, before declining. After four weeks back in neutral range, including a new 6 month low, six weeks ago it returned to positive territory.

Restaurant reservations YoY (from Open Table) State of the Restaurant Industry | OpenTable

  • Last week - 7 day average up +13% YoY, vs. +10% one week prior.

Since 2023 this index has been very volatile, swinging between positive, neutral, and negative several times. In general this year the YoY trends have improved, but with a great deal of noise.

Consumer spending

  • Johnson Redbook up +4.9% YoY, 4 week average +5.2% (high 7.4% 4/25/25; 4.8% 2/1 and 7/3, 2025) United States Redbook Index.

The Redbook index has been positive since February last year. It had very good readings in April and early May, probably due to consumers front-running anticipated tariff price increases and shortages. Since then it had been weakening - until three weeks ago, when it apparently stabilized.

Consumer inflation by Truflation (Independent, economic & financial data in real time on-chain | Truflation)

  • Down -0.35% to +1.67% YoY (High 3.11% 12/11/24 - Low 1.06% 9/13/24).

This recent addition is a daily update to inflation, similar to the “billion prices project” of the last decade (which required a subscription). I have not added this to my list below of coincident or leading indicators, but needless to say it is an up-to-the-moment reading on this very important indicator. This series has been volatile, and the YoY comparisons had been turning higher since September, but has been very low in the last several months, although it did make another new two month high nine weeks ago.

Real Consumer Spending

  • Up +3.2% YoY (high - 6.0% 4/25/25; 12 month low 1.7%% 12/16/24).

This metric premiered at the beginning of last year. One of my most important mantras is that consumption leads employment. Real retail sales have a long history of doing so, but are only reported on a monthly basis.

The weekly result is derived simply by subtracting YoY inflation as measured by Truflation from the YoY change in nominal consumer spending as measured by Redbook. While it will be somewhat noisy, it should anticipate changes in the monthly measures ahead of time. It backed off from the 12 month high it set in February, and then gradually rebounded, and made another 12 month high several months ago. After mainly weeks in the upper part of its 12 month range, it was tepid at the beginning of the year, before improving in the past two months, even making a new 12 month high before backing off. With nominal spending relatively low and inflation holding generally steady, this indicator has weakened, although it is still positive. It did improve along with nominal consumer spending in the past month.

Transport

Railroads (from the AAR)

  • Carloads up +0.9% YoY (cumulative 2025 vs. 2024 up +2.7%)
  • Intermodal units up +1.3% YoY (cumulative up +4.8%)
  • Total loads up +1.1% YoY (cumulative up +3.8%).

(Graph at Railfax Report - North American Rail Freight Traffic Carloading Report.)

Shipping transport

  • Harpex up +1 to 2184 (new 24 month high)
  • Baltic Dry Index down -117 to 2003.

Rail was mixed for many weeks before turning positive in March, presumably reflecting businesses attempting to get inventory before the new tariffs kick in. Intermodal rail loads had steadily decelerated since then, and five weeks ago turned negative. The total number of total loads was negative for several weeks as well. All three measures improved sharply so far this month, and remained positive this week.

Harpex was as high as nearly 4500 almost 2 years ago, but declined as low as 810 in December of last year before rising, setting repeated 12 month highs in the past four months. The usual interpretation for this is that demand is straining against supply, showing a strong global economy, and thus very positive.

Similarly, the BDI was in a generally declining trend throughout 2022 before bottoming at a three year low of 530 in February 2023. The overall trend has been higher since then, rising to a high of 3346 last December, probably reflecting the Houthi attacks on shipping in the Red Sea. This year its low was 1309 in January, and its recent high was 2419 last March. It declined to the bottom 1/3rd of its one year range during the rest of the year, but rebounded all the way to the top of its six month range earlier this year, before retreating back into the middle of that range.

I am wary of reading too much into price indexes like this, since they are heavily influenced by supply (as in, a huge overbuilding of ships in the last decade) as well as demand.

Steel production (American Iron and Steel Institute)

  • Up +0.5% w/w
  • Up +4.8% YoY.

This metric has been very volatile for over a year. Recently it improved to neutral and then positive for two weeks before backing off to neutral again in May. Last week its YoY measure was sufficiently small to change the rating to neutral, and two weeks ago it returned to positive.

Summary And Conclusion

Below are this week’s spreadsheets of the long leading, short leading, and coincident readings. Check marks indicate the present reading. If there has been a change this week, the prior reading is marked with an X:

Long leading Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Corporate bonds ✓
10 year Treasury ✓
10 yr-2 yr Treasury ✓
10 yr. - 3 mo. Treasury X ✓
2 yr - Fed funds ✓
Mortgage rates ✓
Purchase Mtg. Apps. ✓
Refi Mtg Apps. ✓
Real Estate Loans ✓
Real M1 ✓
Real M2 ✓
Corporate Profits ✓
Adj. Fin. Conditions Index ✓
Leverage Index ✓
Totals: 5 3 6
Short Leading Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Credit Spread ✓
Miller Score ✓
St. L. Fin. Stress Index ✓
US$ Broad ✓
US$ Major currencies ✓
Total commodities ✓
Industrial commodities x ✓
Stock prices ✓
Regional Fed New Orders ✓
Initial jobless claims ✓
Temporary staffing ✓
Gas prices ✓
Oil prices ✓
Gas Usage ✓
Totals: 8 4 3
Coincident Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Weekly Econ. Index ✓
Open Table ✓
Redbook ✓
Rail ✓
Harpex ✓
BDI ✓
Steel ✓
Tax Withholding ✓
TED & LIBOR (deleted)
SOFR ✓
Financial Cond. Index ✓
Totals: 9 1 0

.

There has been very little change in the overall picture of the data in a number of weeks: long leading indicators are very mixed, short leading indicators positive, and coincident indicators very positive.

Probably the most positive are the stock market and initial jobless claims among the short leading indicators. Until at least one of these weakens, it is hard to see the near term picture being negative. And among the coincident indicators, the two that measure consumer behavior - retail spending and restaurant dining - continue quite positive. Until consumers show signs of pulling back, the coincident picture is likely to be good.

That being said, last week I wrote that “This coming week I will be especially watching to see if consumer spending on goods shows signs of further slowing compared with March and April; and also whether there is payback from last month’s big upward seasonal adjustment in education hiring, which was nearly half of the entire employment gain.” Indeed real spending on goods remained weak compared with March and April, and the June education hiring was completely revised away. The monthly data, as opposed to this weekly data, is at very least flashing yellow, and within the next week could be flashing red, i.e., “recession watch.”

In which regard, the news that broke late Friday that Trump fired the BLS Administrator in response to the weak employment report has so far (as I write this report) been treated as appalling by the financial press. If the reaction to bad news is to immediately fire the messenger, nobody will be able to trust any good news delivered under the new regime. It is fortunate, therefore, that much of the data you see in the above report is generated by private sources or by the Fed, and in at least one case, the States. It appears that cross-checking BLS data with other, non-government sources is likely to become very important starting almost immediately.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Wall Street on Friday suffered its work week since late May, as market participants that had driven stocks to record highs faced a reality check in a combination of trade developments, weak economic data, and concerns over the independence of federal organizations from U.S. President Donald Trump.

The roller-coaster ride for equities began with the conclusion of the Federal Reserve's fifth monetary policy committee meeting on Wednesday. The central bank held its key rate steady, and chair Jerome Powell signaled that policymakers were in no hurry to cut interest rates. Stocks sold off after the Fed's messaging, and traders were quick to dial back rate cut odds for September.

Those odds took another hit on Thursday, and stocks took an additional beating after the release of a hot inflation indicator. The core personal consumption expenditures price index - widely seen as the Fed's preferred inflation gauge - came in a tad hotter than anticipated on a Y/Y basis for June, and the +2.8% reading remains stubbornly higher than the central bank's desired +2% target.

Then came Friday's nonfarm payrolls report. According to government data, U.S. job growth in July was much weaker than anticipated, while payrolls for May and June were collectively revised lower by 258K. Joseph Brusuelas, principal and chief economist at RSM US LLP, dubbed it the "worst major economic report in the post-pandemic era." Rate cut odds for September sharply whipsawed back up to above 80% following the data.

Trump alleged that the jobs data had been "rigged" to make him and the Republican party "look bad." He also said he would fire Bureau of Labor Statistics head Erika McEntarfer after accusing her of faking jobs data before the U.S. election last year.

Turning to trade, this week also marked the reinstatement of reciprocal tariffs on several U.S. trading partners that had been postponed twice since their initial announcement on "Liberation Day" in April.

Amid all the week's headlines, investors kept an eye on the second quarter earnings season as well, which picked up significant steam. Grabbing the spotlight were a mixed bag of quarterly reports from tech titans: Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) and Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) impressed with their performance, Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) disappointed, and Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) largely delivered.

For the week, the S&P (SP500) slid -2.4%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) fell -2.2%. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) slumped -2.9%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.

Seeking Alpha's Calls Of The Week

AppLovin (NASDAQ:APP) Stock Price Presents A Golden Buying Opportunity.

Here's Why Oxford Industries (NYSE:OXM) Is An Attractive Contrarian Pick.
